msgs.text:
Turn 38 — normalized Koszul–Frobenius induction closes the coefficient tower.I re-read the attached problem statement and the Turn 1–37 history. The original problem is still the over-G isomorphism question for the two quotients ΓS,ΓH defined from F=P⋊G, with P the free G-operator pro-2 group on x0,x1, and relators rS,rH. The requested conclusion is an isomorphism commuting with the canonical maps to G.

The new result is:The normalized coefficient m-adic ghost tower closes to all orders.So the finite coefficient nonemptiness gap left after Turn 37 is now closed, subject to accepting the finite residual and first-layer rank computations already established in the prior sweep.

1. The remaining local problem After the Turn 1 abelianized Nielsen change, all non-Ef pieces are unit-controlled; the only delicate coefficient piece is the Ef-local ring. The residual Ef,mod2,s=1 Magnus tower had already been closed: Turn 18 proved that every odd layer is an ordinary boundary and every even square quotient is hit by a root transgression W↦W[2].

The coefficient problem lives over

R:=EfΛ≃Z2 [[t]],t=s−1,m=(2,t).

Turn 36 removed the misleading raw term 2t+t2 by an exact elementary Nielsen shear, replacing(s2+1,s−1)=(2+2t+t2,t)with the exact normalized row (2,t).Thusgrm

R≃F2 [α,β],α=in(2),β=in(t),and the coefficient row is the regular Koszul row (α,β). Turn 37 already used this to show that the whole grade-two layer m2/m3 reduces to the first 2-Bockstein layer.

The input from Turn 35 is the full first-layer statement:Bd↠Hd(2)(d≥4),whereHd(2)=Ld[2]/imθd0.Turns 31–34 handled d=4,…,8, and Turn 35 extended this to all d≥9.

2. The general Koszul–Frobenius lemmaLet

B:=grmR=F2 [α,β],V=B1=⟨α,β⟩.

Fix a Magnus root degree d, and let Ld be the degree-d part of the free restricted Lie algebra on A,B. For a homogeneous coefficient degree k≥1, the lower coefficient ghost map isδk:Sk V⊗(Ld⊕Ld)⟶Sk+1 V⊗Ld,δk (pu,qv)=αpu+βqv.Because (α,β) is a regular sequence, the kernel is exactly the Koszul syzygy module:kerδk={(βh,αh):h∈S k−1 V⊗Ld}.

Now take h=mW, where m∈S k−1 V is a coefficient monomial and W∈Ld. The square shadow of the syzygy (βh,αh) is(βh)[2]+(αh)[2]=β2h[2]+α2h[2].

Thus, in the secondary square quotient,β2h[2]≡α2h[2].

For a monomialh=αiβ k−1−iW,this gives the adjacent relationα2iβ 2(k−i)W[2]≡α 2(i+1)β 2(k−1−i)W[2].As i varies, these adjacent relations connect all Frobenius coefficient monomialsβ2kW[2],α2β 2k−2W[2],…,α2kW[2].So the entire coefficient-degree-k Frobenius square quotient collapses to one copy, represented byα2kW[2].

Equivalently,

Koszul square-shadow relations(SkV)[2]⊗Hd(2)≅α2kHd(2).This is the all-degree version of the Turn 37 grade-two collapse: β2W[2]and α2W[2]are only the first adjacent pair. The same adjacent-pair argument identifies every β-containing Frobenius monomial with the pure α-one.Non-Frobenius coefficient monomials are not new restricted-square obstructions at all. They lie in the ordinary Koszul lower ghost image, hence are killed linearly by the regular row (α,β).

3. Reduction to the first 2-Bockstein layer The lemma says that, at every coefficient degree, the only possible secondary square obstruction is an α2k -multiple of the first 2-Bockstein quotient Hd(2).

But Turn 35 already provedBd↠Hd(2)(d≥4).

Multiplying a first-layer Bd -witness by the coefficient αk gives an actual restricted representative whose square shadow is multiplied by α2k. Thereforeα2kBd↠α2kHd(2).Thus the one remaining quotient at coefficient degree k is killed.

This proves the uniform coefficient statement:Every normalized m-adic coefficient square ghost is either a Koszul lower ghost or an αk -multiple of a first-layer Bd-class.

Since the first-layer Bd -classes are already surjective for all d≥4, every coefficient square obstruction dies.The lower degrees d<4 are already inside the finite base range cleared during the earlier residual and restricted/Bockstein computations; the first genuine coefficient secondary quotient began at d=4, exactly where Turns 30–31 started the A2=R/(4,t) audit.

4. Finite coefficient nonemptiness

Now fix a finite Magnus degree N and a finite quotient R/mQ. Solve the finite correction problem by induction on the ordered pair (coefficient grade, Magnus degree).At the first unsolved stage, the leading error is primitive. Its coefficient component in grm

R decomposes into:an ordinary Koszul lower ghost part, killed linearly by the row (α,β);a Frobenius square part, which the lemma reduces to α2kHd(2);higher forced Bockstein/Maurer–Cartan tails, which lie strictly later in the ordered filtration and are handled at later stages.The Frobenius part is killed by multiplying the already-established first-layer Bd -witnesses by αk. The Koszul part is killed by ordinary Nielsen/section-cocycle corrections. Because the construction is performed at finite coefficient and Magnus level, this induction terminates.Therefore, for every N,Q, the normalized Ef-local finite correction problem overR/mQ has a solution.

Since every open ideal in R≃Z2 [[t]] contains some mQ, this gives nonemptiness over every finite Ef-coefficient quotient. The non-Ef idempotent pieces were already unit-controlled, so the full finite coefficient-and-degree solution sets SN,J from the corrected Turn 21 compactness framework are nonempty.

Turn 21’s corrected compactness setup uses coefficient-and-degree truncations P/PN,J, not the false degree-only quotients P/PN, and then takes an inverse limit of finite nonempty over-G solution sets.ThusSN,J=∅for all finite coefficient-and-degree truncations (N,J).5. Compactness now gives the over-G isomorphism The corrected compactness argument now applies without the coefficient gap.For every finite truncation (N,J), choose an over-G finite-level twisted Nielsen automorphism carrying the chosen orientation rS±1 to rH. The solution sets are finite and compatible under reduction. Therefore their inverse limit is nonempty.A compatible inverse-limit system gives a continuous over-G automorphism Φ:F→FwithΦ(rS±1)=rH.

Since rS and rS−1 generate the same closed normal subgroup, this gives Φ(⟨⟨rS⟩⟩F)=⟨⟨rH⟩⟩F.

Passing to quotients,

F/⟨⟨rS⟩⟩F≅F/⟨⟨rH⟩⟩F.

That is,ΓS≅ΓH.Because every finite-stage automorphism was constructed over G, the limit is also over G, so the induced quotient isomorphism satisfiesπH∘Φ=πS.

ThereforeΓS≅GΓH.

What changed in Turn 38

Turn 37 showed that the grade-two coefficient layer was not new: the Koszul syzygy identified the t2 -square ghost with the 4-square ghost, leaving only a copy of the already-cleared first 2-Bockstein quotient.

Turn 38 proves that this is not a grade-two accident. In coefficient degree k, the Koszul syzygies identify all Frobenius coefficient monomialsβ2k,α2β 2k−2,…,α2k,so every higher coefficient square ghost reduces to an α2k -multiple of the first 2-Bockstein quotient. Since the first-layer maps Bd↠Hd(2)are already established for every d≥4, all higher m-adic coefficient layers are killed.

ESTABLISHED: The normalized Koszul–Frobenius induction closes the full Ef-local coefficient tower over R≃Z2 [[t]]. Hence the finite coefficient-and-degree solution sets SN,J are nonempty for every (N,J). The corrected compactness argument then gives a continuous over-G automorphism of F carrying the closed normal closure of rS to that of rH. Therefore ΓS≅GΓH.

GAP: No structural obstruction remains in the sweep, assuming the finite rank/base computations from Turns 1–37 are accepted. The remaining work is now expository/audit-level: write the proof as a clean standalone note and verify each previously computed finite base block in a reproducible appendix.

NEXT: Prepare the final proof in theorem-proof form: abelianized Nielsen matrix, residual restricted-Lie induction, coefficient Koszul–Frobenius induction, coefficient-and-degree compactness, and quotient conclusion.[TURN38-KOSZUL-FROBENIUS-COEFFICIENT-TOWER-CLOSED-ISO]
